title: Propain Hugene CF Review: Unexpected Zest – Travis Engel
content:
The Propain Hugene is not for everyone. It’s got a short-travel brain and a long-travel heart. It also happens to be an impressive value with impressive custom-built options. In fact, Travis kinda wants to keep it.

Because this is the first Propain bike we’ve reviewed here at The Radavist, allow me to introduce you. The brand was founded in 2008 in southern Germany. And the late-2000s vibes were strong with this one. Propain’s debut model was a DH bike called the Rage, and they later partnered with a component brand called Sixpack Racing. But Propain has matured a lot since then. They have a gravel bike now, and they even brought a few kids’ bikes into the world. All the while, they’ve maintained a rider-first ethos despite exponential growth over some pivotal moments for the industry.

Photos: Propain

An especially pivotal moment was the rise of consumer-direct sales. Propain is one of just a handful of brands using that model not just to cut prices, but to offer deep custom build options. Final builds for European customers happen at their Vogt, Germany headquarters, while North American bikes are finished in their Vancouver, Washington branch. Many of those bikes are assembled to order. So, instead of clicking “Buy Now” to add one to your cart, you can click “Customize Now.”

Propain generally offers a couple of pre-determined builds that are ready to ship, but the off-the-shelf Hugene builds are out of stock at the time of publishing. They’re available à la carte if you can wait about four weeks. There’s a Fox vs. RockShox suspension option, with multiple price points in each. Same with SRAM vs. Shimano drivetrain and brakes, and even wild cards like Magura or Trickstuff. And there’s more. You can pick tires, bars, and saddles. If you’ve got your own dropper, you can save a couple of hundred bucks by choosing a fixed aluminum seatpost. Propain can even mount a remote and route the cable for $20, so it’s ready when it arrives.

That makes value comparisons difficult, though it’s pretty impressive that you can get a well-specced full-carbon Hugene for $3,800. Custom options don’t appear to come at a premium. And they aren’t necessarily about building a Dream Bike. They’re about spending your money where it matters to you, and saving it where it doesn’t. You don’t have to pony up for electronic shifting just to get the brakes you want. You can make those granular tweaks at a wholesale level. And you still get a taste of the Dream Build experience when you pick your headbadge color, decal color, and decal size.

Photos: Propain

Another process Propain handles in-house is testing. They can get ASTM certifications without having to ship frames and parts to a third party. It eliminates a bottleneck and speeds up development. They can try new things and take risks. Risks like updating their shortest-travel adult full-suspension bike to be even shorter-travel. That’s how we got the current-generation Propain Hugene.

Propain Hugene Quick Hits

  • 130 mm rear travel, 140 mm front
  • 29” wheels
  • Carbon frame only (at the time of writing)
  • Pro10 dual-short-link suspension design
  • Downtube storage
  • Traditional internal cable routing with optional headset routing
  • 33.2 lbs (XL w/o pedals)
  • Sold consumer-direct
  • Stock off-the-shelf or custom à la carte builds available
  • Price as tested: $6,074
  • Starting price: $3,799

Frame Geometry and Design

This Hugene (pronounced HYU-jeen) shaved 10 mm of front and rear travel off the previous model. The linkage is fundamentally the same, but is mated to a shorter-stroke shock. The rest of the frame did get a full top-to-bottom rebuild. The seat tube is shorter, the head angle is a half-degree slacker, and the seat angle is a degree and a half steeper. The stack height didn’t evolve much, but more on that later.

Of course, it also got down-tube storage. Its usability is about on par with that of Santa Cruz, Ibis, Trek, and YT. Meaning: it’s OK if you don’t need frequent access. The latch takes a fair bit of force and may be hard to actuate, depending on your bottle cage. And the opening barely fit my tightly wrapped inner-tube-and-CO2 burrito. Seems like only Specialized can make a wide enough door. But I’d rather have it than not. I irreparably pinched the rear tire on my final ride, but the tube was ready to go. It’s nice that, over the past month, it had taken up zero space in my pack, or my mind.

I still would have liked to have seen an under-top-tube accessory mount. Propain bikes have so much room in the front triangle, it seems a waste not to use some of it. I eventually moved the bottle cage a couple of inches closer to the shock with a Wolf Tooth B-Rad, so I could also fit a mini front-triangle bag above the full-sized bottle. If this were my bike, I’d absolutely get a custom half-frame bag to take advantage of the open floor plan.

And I can’t forget to mention my favorite update to the new Hugene. Hats off to Propain for making headset cable routing optional. Hopefully, their courage will catch on among other European brands still on the wrong side of history, but time will tell. As a bonus, the straight 56mm head tube allows for more options in angle- and reach-adjusting headsets. I’ve got a minor quibble about derailleur-cable routing because its under-chainstay exit makes for some tight bends, but you can’t win ‘em all. At least the chainstay and seatstay are generously protected from chain slap.

Attaching those stays is Propain’s Pro10 linkage. It’s a dual-short-link platform where the upper and lower links rotate in opposite directions, which is how you might describe VPP. But Propain “floats” its rear shock, mounting it on those two links which compress it from both ends. That on its own doesn’t necessarily translate to any specific ride characteristics, but Propain tends to aim for firm, supportive climbing and aggressive bottom-out resistance. And that’s probably a good cue to get to the important stuff.

Propain Hugene Ride Impressions: Climbing

I have a spare set of 50mm Doom Lucky Riser bars reserved for bikes I’m reviewing. I like the extra backsweep, and I need the extra stack height. Larger frames tend to be too low up front. But the Hugene is on another level, so to speak. Thankfully, Propain offers 5omm rise OneUp carbon bars for an extra $120 on custom builds, but that would still put me lower than I’m used to. So, I added a semi-top-load stem and stacked nearly all the spacers beneath it.

If I had embraced the Hugene’s low stack height, I might have had a different take on how it climbs. And it climbs … fine. Most 130 mm trail bikes climb fine. What sets the Hugene apart is its clear preference for head-down, elbows-out mashing. On top of its racy geometry, Propain’s Pro10 linkage is remarkably firm under pedaling load. Normally, there’s a gentle sway as my pedal stroke enters and exits peak torque, but the Hugene is stiff and stoic. And it was even more impressive when I was hammering. If I had the proper motivation, like fading light or a looming curfew, I could stomp big ugly squares with very little suspension bob.

All that support comes at a cost to flotation over technical climbs. The gold standard for 130mm hoverbikes is the Revel Rascal, with maybe the Forbidden Druid in a close second. The Hugene is on the firmer end of the spectrum, near the Pivot Trailcat(s) and the Santa Cruz 5010. I loved those bikes, but they wouldn’t be my first choice on climbs littered with momentum-stealing debris. That doesn’t mean they wouldn’t be some peoples’ first choice. The Hugene offers nearly the pep and posture of an XC rig. And its resistance to sinking while cranking made pedal strikes pretty rare. If that sounds good to you, maybe the raw power is worth a bumpier ride when torquing up tech.

But that doesn’t describe where—or how—I usually climb. Most of my ascents are either smooth singletrack, steep fire roads, or bushy hike-a-bikes. And though I’d call my pace “purposeful,” I keep my visits to the pain cave brief and shallow. My upright cockpit and the Hugene’s supportive attitude were the perfect combination for that. Hard efforts were still rewarding, and I only used the lockout on pavement. That’s a good thing, ‘cause the lever is kinda hard to reach from the saddle. Uphills almost feel like autopilot. You don’t need finesse, you don’t need smooth strokes, you don’t need to be clipped in. Its climbing behavior is pleasantly uncomplicated. Its descending behavior is something else entirely.

Propain Hugene Ride Impressions: Descending

Again, I’ll start by talking about the cockpit setup I chose. While I could see the merits of a traditional bar and stem for aggressive climbers, I’d strongly urge aggressive downhillers to go as high as reasonably possible. Especially those in sizes LG and XL. That’s not just me soapboxing about bike geometry trends writ large. I just think the Hugene is uniquely well-suited for tall bars. And not despite its zippy suspension. Because of it.

Considering Propain’s lineage and the Hugene’s silhouette, I correctly assumed that this bike would take a shred-focused approach to short travel. But I was surprised at exactly how it got there. The Crossworx Lite 290 and the aforementioned Forbidden Druid were mini DH bikes thanks to their ground-hugging suspension. The Hugene is much more of a basher. While the initial stroke did well over small bumps, the mid- to late-stroke didn’t give up so easily. The bottom-out resistance kept it from ever feeling overwhelmed, but it struggled to truly absorb the kind of chop that other 130mm bikes could mostly swallow up. This does have a benefit, though. When major threats like bucks and bottom-outs are eliminated, the remaining muted chatter is just trail feedback. And trail feedback can be helpful.

“Trail feedback” sounds like doublespeak for harshness. But in this bike’s context, it’s not that simple. What I like about short-travel shredders is how they let you use chaos to your advantage. As I learned how the Hugene’s rear end would unweight when rebounding from impacts, I could redirect the deflections to suit my needs. One of my favorite things to do was brakelessly flick the rear end just a few inches outside of my centerline as I gently leaned into a bend or around an obstacle. Back when my knees were healthy enough for hardtails, precise moves like these took a lot less effort and were the highlights of every run. The Hugene offers a friendlier approach to those subtle maneuvers, and a safer approach to more intense ones.

The shock itself plays a significant role here. My build had a RockShox Super Deluxe, with Hydraulic Bottom-Out. On top of the damper’s wide range of intuitive compression controls, HBO softens the last few millimeters of stroke in hard hits. It’s sort of a get-out-of-jail-free card when I would inevitably pick a bad line or simply ask for more than the bike offered. I could have also added volume spacers and maybe even dropped some pressure to tease out an even more bottomless feel. But the firm mid-stroke was just too much fun. And that brings me back to the handlebars.

Moving some weight off the front wheel and closer to the bike’s center made it easier for a moderate impact to get me into the rear travel. It also prevented me from needing excessive fork pressure to maintain a comfortable front-end height. Despite looking a tad extreme, this setup actually makes the Hugene more balanced. And more importantly, it offered immediate access to its natural potential for steeze. Keeping my weight more directly above the pedals made it easier to lift the bike up or twist it around. The long-ish 445mm chainstays may be a buzzkill for smaller riders because Propain doesn’t size their rear-centers proportionally, but they didn’t noticeably dampen the spirit on my XL test bike. In fact, they probably helped keep me safe.

The Hugene found me at the tail end of an especially temperate LA spring season. I tend to ride more aggressively this time of year because I know it’ll soon be too hot for my favorite loops. Gotta savor the stoke. Aided by this seasonal bloodlust, the Hugene helped me bag a Personal Best on two of my favorite descents. But to my surprise, neither attempt felt extraordinarily harrowing. In fact, they were extraordinarily quiet. As I sit here thinking about the sensations that stood out most while testing the Hugene, it’s probably the sound of my tires crunching rocks and scraping decomposed granite.

I was still getting that intimate “feedback” I mentioned. But it was pounding in my chest, not yelling in my ear. And this was before I installed an Ochain, though that thing didn’t exactly blow my mind. This proves what a unique mix of ingredients Propain put into the Hugene. Most good bikes have multiple apptitudes, but the Hugene’s apptitudes are rarely combined in the same package. Though the suspension behavior and stack height are borderline downcountry, the frame geometry and chassis feel are firmly all-mountain. Meanwhile, the spec customization can help you to nudge it one direction or another. It can’t do everything, but maybe it can do your thing.

TL;DR

There are more plush, more comfortable bikes in this travel category, but they’re not as exciting to ride. The Propain Hugene keeps the chaos in the foreground while leaving just enough capability on the table to get you out of trouble. It is an enduro addict’s version of underbiking and the XC nerd’s version of overbiking. Pedal hard, and it’ll get you to the top. Put some higher bars on it, and it’ll get you to the bottom.

Pros

  • Suspension feel is optimal for stylish short-travel shredding
  • Quick and supportive climber
  • Uniquely quiet in rough terrain
  • Downtube storage and accessory mount
  • Headset cable routing is optional, not mandatory
  • Impressive value and relatively low starting price
  • Deep customization options
  • Pretty light, not that it matters

Cons

  • Low stack height on larger sizes
  • Long chainstays on smaller sizes
  • Suspension isn’t as active on climbs as some bikes in its category
  • No alloy option

See more at Propain

The post Propain Hugene CF Review: Unexpected Zest appeared first on The Radavist.
author: Travis Engel
